skill.name: structured-logging
skill.description: Guidance for designing structured log events, canonical log lines, and wide-event observability practices for application services.
skill.summary: Design wide structured log events that make production debugging faster and more precise.
skill.author: Chemiseblanc

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found they are Markdown formatting, field names, examples, and table text rather than executable commands or malicious code. No prompt injection, network activity, file access, or executable scripts were found. The remaining concern is medium risk because the reference encourages logging privacy-sensitive fields and should be used with data minimization controls.

medium_findings.title: Privacy-Sensitive Logging Guidance
medium_findings.description: The field reference lists client IP address, user agent, user email, payment last four digits, and stack trace as possible log fields. It also warns against passwords, tokens, full card numbers, and PII unless necessary, so this is not malicious, but it can lead to excessive sensitive data collection without retention, masking, and access controls.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines explicitly recommend optional fields that can contain personal or sensitive data. The same document includes a warning against sensitive data logging, which lowers severity and confirms this is a governance risk rather than malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: External Command Alerts Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The external command findings map to fenced code blocks, inline field names, logging examples, and Markdown tables. I found no executable skill script, shell invocation, Ruby backtick execution, or user-controlled command construction in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files are Markdown documentation and the cited lines are prose, tables, or illustrative pseudocode. There is no runnable command surface in the skill package.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Field-Name False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography findings correspond to words inside the skill description, Markdown table headers, identifiers, and ordinary log-field names. No cryptographic algorithm, hashing code, encryption code, or credential handling implementation is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed context contains documentation terms only. There are no crypto APIs, security-sensitive transforms, or algorithm selections to exploit.

low_findings.title: C2, SQLite, and Reconnaissance Alerts Are Benign Text Matches
low_findings.description: The C2 keyword alert appears inside a JSON log example, the SQLite database alerts are ordinary database timing field names, and reconnaissance alerts are guidance lines about log fields. No command-and-control behavior, database file access, or host reconnaissance logic was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited evidence is static documentation content, not executable code. The package contains only SKILL.md and a Markdown reference file.

content.value_statement: Production debugging is slow when logs are scattered, low context, or hard to query. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code design wide structured events with useful request, user, error, and timing context.
actual_capabilities: Explains the wide-event pattern for one comprehensive log event per request.
Shows what context belongs in request, user, business, error, timing, infrastructure, and feature flag fields.
Compares scattered log statements with a single structured event approach.
Provides pseudocode for request middleware that enriches and emits a final log event.
Gives query examples for investigating failures, latency, and customer-specific behavior.
Warns against logging passwords, tokens, full card numbers, and unnecessary personal data.
limitations: It is guidance only and does not install a logging library or agent.
Examples are generic and must be adapted to each language, framework, and compliance requirement.
It does not define retention policies, access controls, or redaction rules for log storage.
Some suggested fields can contain personal data and require careful filtering.

use_cases.title: Instrument a Web Service
use_cases.description: Design one structured request event that captures request metadata, outcome, latency, and domain context.

use_cases.title: Improve Incident Debugging
use_cases.description: Review existing logs and identify missing fields that block fast production investigation.

use_cases.title: Create Logging Standards
use_cases.description: Define consistent field names, severity rules, and privacy boundaries for service teams.

prompt_templates.title: Review One Log Event
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a single log statement
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this log event for structured logging quality. Identify missing request, user, error, timing, and business fields. Also flag any sensitive data that should not be logged.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Wide Event
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a wide structured log event for this endpoint. Include field groups for request context, business context, dependencies, timing, errors, and feature flags. Explain which fields are required and which are optional.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor Scattered Logs
prompt_templates.scenario: Convert many logs into one canonical event
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor these scattered log statements into one canonical request event. Preserve useful debugging context, remove duplicate messages, and add privacy-safe field names.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Logging Standard
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a structured logging standard for this service family. Include severity rules, required fields, naming conventions, redaction guidance, sampling rules, and example investigation queries.

output_examples.input: A checkout endpoint logs start, user lookup, cart lookup, payment retry, and final failure as separate messages.
output: A single request event with checkout path, status, duration, retry count, payment error category, cart value, and feature flag state.
A privacy note to avoid full payment details and unnecessary personal identifiers.
Suggested queries for failed premium-user checkout attempts and slow payment providers.

output_examples.input: An API service logs only a generic database timeout message.
output: A fuller event design with request ID, operation name, table or resource, query duration, timeout threshold, row count estimate, and retry status.
A recommendation to include enough context for debugging without storing raw query parameters that may contain private data.

output_examples.input: A team wants a standard for log levels and field names across services.
output: Clear level rules for successful requests, degraded requests, and failed requests.
A shared naming convention for request, user, timing, dependency, error, and deployment fields.
A checklist for redaction, retention, and access review before production rollout.

best_practices: Emit one enriched event at the end of each request or job.
Use consistent field names and data types across services.
Classify and redact sensitive values before logs leave the application.
anti_patterns: Writing many small log lines that require manual reconstruction.
Logging only low-cardinality fields such as status and level.
Adding emails, tokens, payment data, or stack traces without a clear policy.

faq.question: Does this skill run code or collect logs?
faq.answer: No. It is a Markdown guidance skill and does not execute code, access systems, or collect log data.

faq.question: What is a wide event?
faq.answer: A wide event is one structured record with many useful fields about a request, job, or operation.

faq.question: Can I use this with any logging library?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance is library-neutral and can be adapted to common logging frameworks.

faq.question: Should I log user email or IP address?
faq.answer: Only when necessary. Prefer stable internal identifiers, masking, retention limits, and restricted access.

faq.question: How does this help debugging?
faq.answer: It makes logs easier to query by request, user, error category, dependency, feature flag, and latency.

faq.question: Does it replace tracing or metrics?
faq.answer: No. It complements traces and metrics by adding rich, queryable event context.
